retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Pelvic Inlet -- Other Issues -- Pelvimetry and Obstetrics: Past and Present
retrieved_docs.content: The evidence illuminated by the radiological studies supports the research and definition of a clinical test of external pelvimetry. The present qualitative test in pelvimetry, currently suggested in obstetric and osteopathic settings, has some practical limitations like the lack of standardization, the difficulty for most pregnant women near term gestation to come down smoothly into a squatting position, and the lack of reference curves and cut-off values.

retrieved_docs.title: Chandelier Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Chandelier sign is a colloquial term for cervical motion tenderness (CMT). The term refers to the intense pain reaction experienced by patients with CMT, as if they are leaping off the examination table toward a chandelier on the ceiling. CMT is a gynecological examination finding that can be indicative of peritoneal infection. This sign is a critical clinical finding that can be assessed in female patients of various ages and can significantly influence a differential diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Physical Principles And Instrumentation -- Definition/Introduction -- Instrumentation
retrieved_docs.content: Endocavity probes are one of the most unique in appearance. The wand-like shape and long neck allow sonographers to extend into orifices such as the oropharynx, rectum, or vagina (also called a transvaginal probe). Common clinical applications include surveillance of early pregnancies. Transabdominal imaging may not provide optimal imaging in smaller fetuses, but it can be diagnosed and used as a therapeutic tool in draining parapharyngeal abscesses. See Image. Endocavity Ultrasound Probe.

retrieved_docs.title: Trendelenburg Sign -- Definition/Introduction -- Prerequisites of Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The patient can stand on the affected side for more than 30 seconds The patient can understand commands The patient has intact coordination The patient has free abduction movement; no fixed adduction deformity

retrieved_docs.title: Forceps Delivery -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Absolute maternal contraindications for operative vaginal delivery include the following: Cervix not fully dilated Membranes intact Fetal head not engaged Unknown fetal position Cephalopelvic disproportion Relative maternal contraindications include malpresentation (unless planned breech extraction) and connective tissue disorders Absolute fetal contraindications include the presence of a bleeding disorder (hemophilia, thrombocytopenia, von Willebrand disease) or bone demineralization (osteogenesis imperfecta) Relative fetal contraindications include prematurity and macrosomia. There is no consensus on the minimum or maximum estimated fetal weight for forceps delivery.[9]

retrieved_docs.title: Vacuum Extraction -- Indications -- Classification of Operative Vaginal Deliveries
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction is classified by the status of the fetal head at the time of vacuum application and the degree of rotation necessary. Outlet delivery The scalp is visible at the introitus without separating the labia. The fetal skull has reached the pelvic floor. The fetal head is at or on the perineum. The sagittal suture is in AP or ROA/LOA or OP position. Rotation does not exceed 45 degrees. Low delivery The leading point of the fetal skull is at station +2 or more and not on the pelvic floor. Without rotation: Rotation is 45 degrees or less With rotation: Rotation is greater than 45 degrees Mid delivery The station is above +2, but the head is engaged.

retrieved_docs.title: Halo Brace -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Examples of definitive treatment are occipital condyle fractures, occipitocervical dislocation, C1 fractures (most common), and C2 fractures, with an anticipated average healing time of 3 to 4 months. [3] Although this technique demonstrates an approximate success rate of 85%, the effectiveness of the halo brace hinges on appropriate indication, application, and management. [4] Risks are associated with the use of halo braces as a definitive treatment, especially among older patients, necessitating caution in specific populations. [5] In addition, this device can be used in the pediatric population for cervical spine trauma (definitive or conjunction with surgical management), severe scoliosis, and arthrodesis, although this entails adjustments like utilizing more pins and applying reduced insertion torque force to accommodate differences in skull thickness.

retrieved_docs.title: Evidence-Based Medicine -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Level IV includes expert opinions from respected authorities on the subject based on their clinical experience.

retrieved_docs.title: Hip Precautions -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Total hip arthroplasty (THA) continues to rank as one of the most successful surgical treatments for end-stage hip osteoarthritis. [1] [2] Other indications for THA include hip osteonecrosis (ON), congenital hip disorders, including hip dysplasia, post-traumatic arthritis, and inflammatory arthritic conditions. Hip ON, on average, presents in the younger patient population (35 to 50 years of age) and accounts for approximately 10% of annual THAs. [3] Given the consistent, reproducible documented success of THA over the last several decades, the popularity of this procedure has increased with a projected growth of 170% by the year 2030 and more than 193000 performed annually in the US. [4] Hip precautions, in general, represent a well-established post-operative protocol to mitigate the risks of THA dislocations, which currently occur at an estimated total incidence rate of 1% to 3. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Modified Ashworth Scale -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: 0: No increase in muscle tone 1: Slight increase in muscle tone, with a catch and release or minimal resistance at the end of the range of motion when an affected part(s) is moved in flexion or extension 1+: Slight increase in muscle tone, manifested as a catch, followed by minimal resistance through the remainder (less than half) of the range of motion 2: A marked increase in muscle tone throughout most of the range of motion, but affected part(s) are still easily moved 3: Considerable increase in muscle tone, passive movement difficult 4: Affected part(s) rigid in flexion or extension

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Ring Injuries -- Complications -- Malunion
retrieved_docs.content: Controversy exists surrounding the definition or measurement of displacement and malunion in pelvic ring injuries. Patients who achieve anatomic reductions tend to have better outcomes, although displacements less than 1 cm are often considered tolerable. Distinguishing stable patterns from unstable patterns can sometimes be challenging. In select cases or to guide treatment during surgery, manipulation under anesthesia may be considered to determine stability. Nonoperative treatment of complete sacral fractures with ipsilateral rami fractures may lead to displacement over time in 39% of cases, while the rate increases to 68% for those with bilateral rami fractures. [40] [41]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS USA Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act -- Issues of Concern -- Patients who are found to have an "emergency medical condition" must be "stabilized" and treated within the facility's capabilities. If definitive treatment cannot be provided after stabilization, an "appropriate" transfer may take place to another facility with adequate capabilities.
retrieved_docs.content: According to the language used in the EMTALA legislation, stabilization of a patient involves providing [see Federal statute 42 USCS § 1395dd section e(3)(A)]: "Such medical treatment of the condition as may be necessary to assure, within reasonable medical probability, that no material deterioration of the condition is likely to result from or occur during the transfer of the individual from a facility." For a pregnant woman with contractions, the term "stabilization" refers to the delivery of the fetus and the placenta. [2] The "transfer" of a patient to another facility refers to the physical movement of the patient. An "appropriate" transfer is one in which the patient receives adequate stabilization to minimize risk during transfer, and the receiving hospital accepts the transfer of the patient. Furthermore, the receiving hospital must have the necessary resources (ie, beds, equipment) and personnel to treat the patient. An "appropriate" transfer also necessitates the transportation of pertinent medical records and diagnostic findings (ie, laboratory values, radiographs, computed tomography [CT] scans). Furthermore, the patient must be transported with qualified personnel and transportation equipment. According to EMTALA, the term "transfer" can also refer to the discharge of the patient from the ED once their emergency medical condition has been stabilized and treated. If the patient is transferred before appropriate stabilization, then the hospital and EMS system transporting the patient can be penalized for violating EMTALA regulations. If the patient is determined to be "stable" and "not in active labor," then the hospital has no further obligations under EMTALA law.

retrieved_docs.title: Quality Assurance -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Professionals train for competency in quality assurance at all levels of patient care delivery. Quality measurement efforts have even become intertwined with reimbursement within the United States healthcare system to ensure providers take certain actions. This paradigm represents a shift in payment policy towards ensuring the quality of care rather than the incredibly complex task of assigning value to care. Some examples of quality metrics include surgical wound infection rates, rate of hospital readmission within 30 days, operative mortality rates, maternal-fetal mortality, nosocomial infections, vaccination rates based on population demographic, rate of patients referred to appropriate screening tests as defined by the United States Preventative Services Task Force (USPSTF), number of diabetic patients meeting goal hemoglobin A1c <7%, and proper timing of inpatient medication administration. [4] [5] [6] [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Evidence-Based Medicine -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Level IIB evidence is from at least one well-designed case-control or cohort study. A randomized controlled study cannot effectively or ethically study all clinical questions.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Physical Principles And Instrumentation -- Definition/Introduction -- Instrumentation
retrieved_docs.content: The linear transducer does not directly utilize every crystal, but each element is electrically stimulated or vibrated and subsequently excites adjacent crystals. Each crystal within the linear transducer is not given direct electrical stimulation. The advantage of the linear probe is a larger field of view and a higher superficial focus. Common clinical applications of this probe are within the soft tissue and more superficial structures. See Image. Ultrasound Linear Probe.

These indications guide clinical decision‑making throughout pregnancy, labor, and delivery, and they are consistently represented across Dutch and Arabic terminology, ensuring unified understanding and management of maternal health risks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2343
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition and malpresentation of fetus is categorized as a specific condition within the broader domain of normal delivery and other indications for care in pregnancy, labor, and delivery, indicating that it is considered a child concept of this comprehensive obstetric care category. This relationship highlights that malposition and malpresentation are recognized as particular scenarios that fall under the general guidelines and considerations for managing normal deliveries and related clinical indications during pregnancy and childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2733
retrieved_docs.content: Internal and combined version without extraction is a specific form of internal obstetrical version; it represents an obstetric maneuver in which the fetus is turned internally without extraction and is classified under the broader category of internal obstetrical versions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Combined obstetrical version is a category of obstetric procedures, and internal and combined version without extraction is a specific type within this category, indicating that the latter is a subclass of the former. This relationship clarifies that internal and combined version without extraction falls under the broader concept of combined obstetrical version.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2078
retrieved_docs.content: Breech delivery (procedure) is a type of childbirth in which the fetus presents feet or buttocks first, and it is referred to in Arabic as "ولادة بمجيء مِقعديّ". The English term and the Arabic phrase describe the same obstetric situation, linking the clinical concept of a breech presentation with its linguistic counterpart. Recognizing this relationship helps clinicians and patients who use different languages to identify and manage breech births effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2329
retrieved_docs.content: Breech delivery (procedure) and Other partial breech extraction are both linked to the Arabic term ولادة بمجيء مِقعديّ, indicating that they represent the same clinical concept of a breech birth. This relationship underscores that the procedure of delivering a fetus in breech presentation and the specific technique of partial breech extraction are described by the same Arabic nomenclature, facilitating cross‑language understanding in obstetric practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2333
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery” includes a range of obstetric interventions used to facilitate childbirth, and within this group the specific condition “فشل التوليد بالملقط” (failure of delivery with forceps) is classified as a child concept. This relationship indicates that forceps‑related delivery failure is considered a particular outcome or complication associated with the broader set of delivery‑inducing or assisting procedures. Recognizing “فشل التوليد بالملقط” as a subcategory of “Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery” helps clinicians organize risk assessment, anticipate potential complications, and apply appropriate management strategies for assisted vaginal births.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2521
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are specific manifestations that fall under the broader concept of uterine function. Uterine function also includes the conditions الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation) and رحم ناقص التوتر (a uterus with reduced tone). Early placental separation is clinically associated with بضع الرحم (uterine atony), indicating that loss of uterine tone can predispose to premature detachment of the placenta. Recognizing how these forms of asynclitism, uterine functional abnormalities, and their clinical associations interrelate helps clinicians anticipate delivery complications and manage uterine tone to improve maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.content: positioning methods, and a spectrum of complications—including cervical dystocia, uterine atony, prolonged or stimulated labor, and pelvic‑malformation‑related difficult deliveries—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding labor‑related care and its associated indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1850
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pregnancy (حمل منتبذ) is clinically associated with laparotomy (شق البطن), because surgical entry into the abdominal cavity is frequently required for the diagnosis, evaluation, and definitive management of this rare ectopic gestation.

This integrated view aids clinicians in recognizing how detailed pelvic pathologies relate to general obstetric care needs and possible adverse fetal events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2241
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is recognized as a fetal procedure and is classified within a broader parent category of obstetric interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 136
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine function is a parent concept that includes anterior and posterior asynclitism, early placental separation (الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة), uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and uterine prolapse (بضع الرحم). Early placental separation is clinically linked to uterine prolapse, illustrating the connection between placental detachment and secondary uterine dysfunction. Associated obstetric conditions comprise prolonged pregnancy, prolonged first‑stage labor (مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض), prolonged second‑stage labor (مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض), placenta accreta, malignant uterine fibroid (رحى عدارية خبيثة), and abdominal pregnancy. Intrapartum techniques classified as perinatal‑terminology concepts include labor induction (تحفيز المخاض), amniotomy at delivery, intrapartum fetal stimulation, manual fetal rotation, and low forceps delivery with episiotomy—a specific type of vaginal delivery (بضع الفرج). Vaginal birth after cesarean

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are distinct subtypes of uterine function, each describing a specific asynclitic presentation of the fetal head during labor. Both conditions are considered child concepts within the broader category of uterine function, reflecting variations in fetal head alignment that can influence the mechanics of childbirth and guide obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: term malposition/malpresentation within obstetric terminology. These findings reflect عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين (instability of fetal position) and are used in clinical guidelines for “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery” to guide recognition, classification, and management, which may include specialized labor management, operative delivery, cesarean section, or intensified monitoring to protect maternal and neonatal safety. Cephalopelvic disproportion (عدم تناسب الرأس والحوض) is a child concept of Fetal Positions and Presentations and is further connected to structural fetal anomalies such as fetal myelomeningocele and fetal brain hydrocephalus (استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب) that cause the mismatch. The fetal period is also associated with pathological states including foetale ascites (wanverhouding; foetale ascites), foetale hydrops (wanverhouding; foetale hydrops), and other fetal period pathologies that can contribute to abnormal presentations.
